faults.name: Intake Camshaft Position Actuator Circuit (Bank 1)
probable_causes: Open or short in wiring harness to cam actuator
Low oil pressure or sludge blocking oil control valve
fix_steps: Check engine oil level and condition — sludge is the #1 cause
Inspect wiring and connector at the cam position actuator solenoid
Measure solenoid resistance — should be 6-13 ohms typically
Command solenoid on with scan tool and listen for click
Replace solenoid if no click or resistance out of range
If wiring OK and solenoid OK, suspect ECM driver
faults.field_tricks: On GM trucks, try an oil change with a good flush first. 60% of the time the solenoid screen is just clogged with sludge. Pop the solenoid out and clean the screen with brake cleaner before condemning it.

faults.name: Intake Camshaft Position Timing Over-Advanced (Bank 1)
probable_causes: Engine oil too thick, too thin, or overdue for change
Camshaft phaser stuck in advanced position
Oil control valve (VVT solenoid) stuck open
fix_steps: Verify correct oil viscosity and level — change oil if overdue
Monitor cam timing with scan tool — compare actual vs desired
Test VVT solenoid by commanding off — timing should return to base
Check oil pressure with mechanical gauge at operating temp
If timing chain has more than 3 degrees of slack at idle, replace chain and guides
Replace cam phaser if solenoid and oil are confirmed good
faults.field_tricks: On Ford 5.4L 3V, this code plus a rattle at startup means the cam phaser is done. Don't waste time — phasers, chains, guides, and tensioners all go at once. It's a kit job.

faults.name: Intake Camshaft Position Timing Over-Retarded (Bank 1)
probable_causes: VVT solenoid stuck closed or restricted
Low oil pressure at cam phaser
Clogged oil passages in cylinder head
fix_steps: Check oil level and condition first
Remove and inspect VVT solenoid screen for debris
Command solenoid with scan tool — watch cam timing change on live data
Verify oil pressure at cam phaser feed port
If phaser locked retarded, replace phaser assembly
faults.field_tricks: Toyota 2GR-FE V6 loves to throw this after sitting for a while. Start with the oil change and let it idle 15 minutes. If code comes back after re-learning, then dig deeper.

faults.name: Crankshaft/Camshaft Position Correlation (Bank 1 Sensor A)
probable_causes: VVT system stuck at wrong position
fix_steps: Capture crank and cam sensor waveforms with scope — check correlation
Check for excessive timing chain slack by removing valve cover and pressing on chain
Verify VVT system operation — command full advance and retard
If chain jumped one tooth: replace chain, guides, tensioners as a set
Check crank and cam reluctor wheels for damaged teeth
faults.field_tricks: On GM 2.4L Ecotec, a rattle at startup + P0016 = timing chain stretch. Always replace the chain, guides, tensioner, and upper guide bolt (it strips). About a 4-hour job on those.

faults.name: HO2S Heater Control Circuit (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
probable_causes: Failed O2 sensor heater element (open circuit)
Blown fuse for O2 heater circuit
Broken wire in harness — common from exhaust heat damage
fix_steps: Check O2 heater fuse first — replace if blown, check for short if it blows again
Measure heater element resistance at sensor connector — should be 2-30 ohms depending on make
Check for 12V battery voltage on heater supply wire with key on
Inspect connector for melted or corroded terminals
Replace O2 sensor if heater resistance is infinite (open)
faults.field_tricks: Before throwing a new O2 sensor at it, unplug the connector and look for green corrosion. A little dielectric grease on the connector pins saves comeback headaches.

faults.name: Mass Air Flow (MAF) Circuit Malfunction
probable_causes: Contaminated MAF sensor element (oil or dirt)
Air leak between MAF sensor and throttle body
Corroded or loose connector at MAF
fix_steps: Inspect MAF sensor for contamination — clean with MAF-specific cleaner only
Check for intake boot cracks or loose clamps between MAF and throttle body
Verify MAF signal with scan tool — should read 2-7 g/s at idle, 15-25 g/s at 2500 RPM (4-cyl)
Wiggle test wiring and connector while watching live data
If cleaning doesn't fix it, replace MAF sensor
faults.field_tricks: Never touch the MAF element with anything — not your finger, not a brush. Only use CRC MAF cleaner spray. Let it air dry 10 minutes before reinstalling. On Fords with a screen on the air filter housing, remove it — it restricts flow and contaminates the MAF.

faults.name: Mass Air Flow Circuit Range/Performance
probable_causes: Air leak after MAF sensor (cracked intake boot)
fix_steps: Clean MAF sensor with MAF cleaner spray
Check all intake boots and connections for leaks — spray brake cleaner around joints, listen for RPM change
Replace air filter if dirty
Check PCV valve and hoses for leaks or blockage
Compare MAF reading to calculated MAF — if off by more than 10%, replace sensor
faults.field_tricks: Smoke test the intake. Seriously. Half the time this code is a cracked boot or loose clamp you can't see. A $30 smoke machine adapter pays for itself in one job.

faults.name: Mass Air Flow Circuit Low Input
probable_causes: Open circuit in MAF signal wire
MAF sensor unplugged or loose connector
Failed MAF sensor — no signal output
fix_steps: Verify MAF connector is fully seated
Check MAF signal voltage — should be 0.5-1.0V at idle (not near 0V)
Inspect wiring from MAF to ECM for open circuit
Check air filter for restriction
Swap with known-good MAF if available
faults.field_tricks: On GM trucks, the MAF connector lock tab breaks all the time. Vibration backs it out just enough to lose signal intermittently. Zip-tie the connector if the lock is broken.

faults.name: Intake Air Temperature Circuit High Input
probable_causes: Open circuit in IAT sensor wiring
fix_steps: Check IAT connector — verify it's plugged in
Measure IAT resistance — should be 2-3k ohms at 70F ambient
Check for 5V reference and good ground at sensor connector
Inspect wires for breaks — especially where harness flexes
Replace sensor if resistance is infinite
faults.field_tricks: IAT is often built into the MAF on newer cars. If you replace the MAF and this code is still there, you've got a wiring issue, not a sensor issue.

faults.name: Throttle Position Sensor Circuit Malfunction
probable_causes: Signal wire open or shorted to ground
fix_steps: Check TPS voltage at idle — should be 0.4-0.9V
Slowly open throttle while watching voltage — should increase smoothly to 4.5V
Any dropouts or jumps = bad TPS or carbon track
Verify 5V reference and ground at connector
On electronic throttle bodies, often the whole TB must be replaced
faults.field_tricks: If the engine surges or won't hold idle, graph the TPS voltage. Even a tiny 50ms dropout will cause a stumble. The old volt meter won't catch it — you need live graphing.

faults.name: Throttle Position Sensor Range/Performance
probable_causes: Worn TPS (dead spots in carbon track)
Carbon buildup on throttle plate affecting closure
Loose throttle body mounting bolts (vacuum leak)
Wiring issue between TPS and ECM
fix_steps: Clean throttle body plate and bore with TB cleaner
Check TPS voltage sweep — any flat spots or jumps need new TPS
Verify APP sensor tracks smoothly with pedal input
Check for vacuum leaks at throttle body gasket
Relearn idle after cleaning TB — key on 30 sec, start, idle 3 min
faults.field_tricks: After cleaning a drive-by-wire throttle body, you MUST do a throttle relearn or the car will idle weird. Every manufacturer has a different procedure — look it up before you hand it back.

faults.name: Coolant Thermostat Below Regulating Temperature
probable_causes: Low coolant level causing slow warmup
Aftermarket thermostat with wrong temperature rating
fix_steps: Monitor coolant temp with scan tool — should reach 190-220F within 5-10 min of driving
If temp never reaches operating range, thermostat is stuck open — replace it
Verify ECT sensor reading matches actual temp (infrared thermometer on housing)
Check coolant level — air pockets cause false readings
Always install OEM-spec thermostat (correct temp rating)
faults.field_tricks: This is a smog failure code in many states. Replace thermostat, clear code, drive it through a complete warm-up cycle. Make sure the upper radiator hose gets hot — if it's hot right away, thermostat is open.

faults.name: O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
probable_causes: Exhaust leak before upstream O2 sensor
O2 sensor heater not working (slow response)
fix_steps: Check for exhaust leaks at manifold gasket and upstream pipe
Monitor O2 sensor voltage — should toggle 0.1-0.9V at operating temp
If stuck below 0.3V, check for vacuum leaks first
Verify sensor heater is working (should reach operating temp in 30 seconds)
Replace O2 sensor if confirmed stuck lean with no vacuum leaks present
faults.field_tricks: If both bank 1 and bank 2 O2 sensors are reading lean, it's NOT the sensors. It's a system lean condition — check fuel pressure, MAF, and vacuum leaks before replacing sensors.

faults.name: O2 Sensor Circuit Slow Response (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
probable_causes: Exhaust leak near sensor causing false air readings
Fuel contamination (silicone sealant, excessive oil burning)
Coolant intrusion in exhaust (head gasket leak)
Marginal fuel injector causing inconsistent mixture
fix_steps: Graph O2 sensor switching rate — should cross 0.45V 6-8 times in 10 seconds
If switching rate is less than 4 times in 10 seconds, sensor is lazy
Check for exhaust leaks that could dilute exhaust gas
Inspect spark plugs for signs of coolant or oil contamination
Replace O2 sensor if confirmed slow — use OEM or Denso/Bosch
faults.field_tricks: Never use RTV silicone sealant near an O2 sensor. The outgassing from silicone permanently coats the sensor element. Use OEM gaskets or sensor-safe sealant only.

faults.name: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
probable_causes: Burned out O2 sensor heater element
Corroded connector — heat damage from exhaust
ECM heater relay or driver failure
fix_steps: Check heater fuse
Measure heater resistance — typically 2-15 ohms (varies by make)
Verify battery voltage on heater power wire
Verify ECM provides ground for heater circuit
If heater element is open (infinite resistance), replace sensor
faults.field_tricks: On Toyotas, the O2 heater fuse is labeled 'EFI' in the underhood fuse box — not labeled 'O2.' Wastes 20 minutes every time if you don't know that.

faults.name: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
probable_causes: Corroded connector from road splash and heat cycling
Wiring damage from road debris or undercar work
fix_steps: Check fuse for downstream O2 heater circuit
Measure heater resistance at sensor connector — should be 2-15 ohms
Inspect connector for corrosion (downstream sensors are exposed to road spray)
Check for 12V supply and ECM ground at connector
Replace sensor if heater is open circuit
faults.field_tricks: Downstream O2 sensors live in a rough environment. When replacing, always coat the connector with dielectric grease and make sure the rubber boot is intact. Saves a comeback.

faults.name: System Too Lean (Bank 1)
probable_causes: Vacuum leak (intake manifold gasket, PCV hose, brake booster line)
Weak fuel pump or clogged fuel filter
Dirty or failed MAF sensor reading low
fix_steps: Check long-term fuel trim at idle and at 2500 RPM — if high at idle but normal at cruise, it's a vacuum leak
Smoke test intake for vacuum leaks
Clean MAF sensor with MAF cleaner
Check fuel pressure — should be 55-62 PSI on most port injection systems
Inspect PCV valve, brake booster hose, and all small vacuum lines
Check for cracked intake manifold (plastic manifolds crack with age)
faults.field_tricks: If LTFT is +15% at idle but drops to +5% at 2500 RPM, that's a vacuum leak pattern. If LTFT is high at all RPMs, it's fuel delivery (pump, filter, or regulator). That one rule saves hours of guessing.

faults.name: System Too Rich (Bank 1)
probable_causes: Leaking fuel injector (stuck open or dripping)
Faulty fuel pressure regulator (too high pressure)
Saturated charcoal canister (purge valve stuck open)
Stuck-closed PCV valve causing crankcase pressure
Coolant temp sensor reading colder than actual (causes rich fueling)
fix_steps: Check fuel pressure — should not exceed spec by more than 5 PSI
Pinch off purge valve hose — if STFT drops toward 0%, purge system is leaking
Check MAF reading vs spec — clean if contaminated
Verify ECT sensor reads correct operating temperature
Perform injector leak-down test — pressure should hold after pump stops
Inspect spark plugs for black sooty deposits (rich running confirmation)
faults.field_tricks: Pull dipstick at idle. If it smells like raw fuel, you've got a leaking injector or bad fuel pressure regulator. Gas-diluted oil kills bearings fast — change the oil after fixing the rich condition.

faults.name: System Too Lean (Bank 2)
probable_causes: Vacuum leak on bank 2 side of intake manifold
Bank 2 fuel injectors restricted or clogged
PCV valve or hose leak affecting bank 2
fix_steps: Compare LTFT bank 1 vs bank 2 — if only bank 2 is high, leak is on that side
Smoke test focusing on bank 2 intake runners and lower manifold
Check fuel injector flow rates — compare bank to bank
Inspect lower intake manifold gasket (common on GM 3.8L, Ford 4.6L)
Check for cracked plastic intake manifold
faults.field_tricks: On GM 3.8L V6, the lower intake manifold gasket leaks coolant AND air. You'll see P0171 + P0174 together with coolant loss. Replace both upper and lower gaskets as a set — the Dorman updated gasket kit fixes the design flaw.

faults.name: Fuel Rail Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
probable_causes: Low fuel supply from tank pump
Leaking fuel rail or injector seals
fix_steps: Read fuel rail pressure on scan tool — compare desired vs actual
Check low-side fuel pressure first (tank pump output)
Verify high-pressure pump output reaches spec under load
Inspect fuel rail pressure sensor connector and wiring
Replace high-pressure pump if output cannot reach target under load
faults.field_tricks: On GDI engines, always check the low-pressure side first. A weak tank pump makes the high-pressure pump look bad. Minimum 55 PSI on the low side or the HP pump can't build enough rail pressure.

faults.name: Injector Circuit Malfunction
probable_causes: Failed fuel injector coil (open or shorted)
Wiring harness damage at injector connector
fix_steps: Identify which cylinder(s) with cylinder-specific codes (P0201-P0208)
Measure injector resistance — typically 12-16 ohms for high impedance
Check for 12V supply at injector connector with key on
Verify ECM pulse signal with noid light or scope
Swap suspect injector with known good cylinder — see if code follows
faults.field_tricks: Before condemning an injector, try the noid light test. If the light flashes, the ECM side is good and the injector itself is bad. If no flash, it's wiring or ECM — don't waste money on an injector.

faults.name: Engine Coolant Over Temperature
probable_causes: Low coolant level — check for leaks
Failed water pump (impeller eroded or bearing seized)
Blocked radiator (external debris or internal scale)
Failed cooling fan or fan relay
Blown head gasket allowing compression into cooling system
fix_steps: STOP DRIVING — overheating causes warped heads and blown gaskets
Check coolant level when cool — top off and look for leaks
Verify cooling fans operate — command on with scan tool
Check thermostat — remove and test in boiling water, should open at rated temp
Pressure test cooling system — look for external and internal leaks
Block test exhaust gas in coolant — rules out head gasket
faults.field_tricks: Squeeze the upper radiator hose when engine is hot and thermostat should be open. If it's hard and the engine is overheating, the radiator is blocked. If it's soft, the thermostat is closed or water pump is bad.

faults.name: Random/Multiple Cylinder Misfire Detected
probable_causes: Worn spark plugs or incorrect gap
Vacuum leak at intake manifold or hoses
Low fuel pressure causing lean misfire
EGR valve stuck open diluting intake charge
Carbon buildup on intake valves (GDI engines)
fix_steps: Check for accompanying cylinder-specific codes (P0301-P0308)
Inspect spark plugs — check gap, wear, and color
Check fuel pressure under load — should hold steady during snap throttle
Smoke test intake for vacuum leaks
Check compression if plugs, fuel, and air are ruled out
On GDI engines, check for carbon buildup on intake valves with borescope
Verify EGR valve closes fully at idle
faults.field_tricks: If P0300 shows up with a lean code (P0171/P0174), fix the lean condition first. Lean misfires cause P0300 and replacing plugs and coils won't fix it. Vacuum leak or fuel pressure — always check those before ignition parts.

faults.name: Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected
probable_causes: Fuel injector 1 clogged or electrically failed
Low compression on cylinder 1 (valve, ring, or gasket issue)
Wiring issue to coil or injector
fix_steps: Swap coil from cyl 1 to another cylinder — see if misfire follows
If misfire follows coil, replace coil
If misfire stays on cyl 1, swap spark plug
If still misfiring, check injector — swap or test with noid light
If ignition and fuel are good, run compression test
Low compression: leak-down test to determine valves vs rings vs gasket
faults.field_tricks: The coil swap test saves more time than any other trick. Move the suspect coil to a known-good cylinder. If the misfire moves, you found it. Takes 5 minutes and costs nothing.

faults.name: Cylinder 2 Misfire Detected
probable_causes: Wiring damage to coil or injector
fix_steps: Swap coil from cyl 2 to another cylinder
If misfire follows, replace coil
If misfire stays, swap spark plug
Check injector with noid light or swap test
Compression test if ignition and fuel are confirmed good
faults.field_tricks: Same coil swap strategy as any single-cylinder misfire. Document which cylinder the misfire follows to — that's your definitive answer.

faults.name: Cylinder 3 Misfire Detected
probable_causes: Intake manifold gasket leak near cylinder 3 runner
fix_steps: Swap coil from cyl 3 to another cylinder
If misfire follows, replace coil
If misfire stays, swap spark plug
Check injector with noid light or swap test
Compression test if ignition and fuel confirmed good
faults.field_tricks: On V-engines, cylinder 3 is often hard to reach. Use a universal joint on your socket extension to get the coil bolt and plug out without fighting the firewall.

faults.name: Cylinder 4 Misfire Detected
fix_steps: Coil swap test to another cylinder
Spark plug inspection and swap
Injector test with noid light
Compression and leak-down test if others check out
On GDI engines, borescope intake valves for carbon
faults.field_tricks: On 4-cylinder engines, cylinder 4 is closest to the firewall and tends to run the hottest. Plug wear on #4 is often worse than the others — always check plug gap on a single-misfire code.

faults.name: Knock Sensor 1 Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1)
probable_causes: Wiring harness damage — routed near exhaust or accessories
Knock sensor not torqued properly (must be exact spec)
ECM knock sensor input circuit failure
fix_steps: Verify knock sensor connector is clean and fully seated
Measure sensor resistance — typically 100k-500k ohms (varies by design)
Check wiring from sensor to ECM for damage or shorts
If replacing sensor, torque to exact spec (usually 15-20 ft-lbs)
Apply thread sealer if spec calls for it — some require dry install
faults.field_tricks: Knock sensors MUST be torqued to spec. Too tight crushes the piezo element. Too loose and it can't sense vibration. Use a torque wrench, not an impact gun. On Toyotas, it's 29 ft-lbs — use a crowfoot.

faults.name: Crankshaft Position Sensor Circuit Malfunction
probable_causes: Damaged reluctor wheel (cracked or missing teeth)
Wiring open or shorted in CKP circuit
Excessive air gap between sensor and reluctor
Oil or metal debris on sensor tip
fix_steps: Check for spark and injector pulse — no CKP signal means no start
Inspect CKP sensor connector and wiring
Measure sensor resistance (if Hall-effect: check for 5V ref and signal)
Check air gap between sensor and reluctor wheel
Inspect reluctor wheel for cracked or missing teeth (if accessible)
Replace sensor — often fixes intermittent no-start or stalling
faults.field_tricks: Intermittent CKP failures often show up hot. Engine starts fine cold, stalls after warming up, won't restart for 20-30 minutes. Pour cold water on the sensor — if it starts, the sensor is heat-sensitive and needs replacing.

faults.name: Camshaft Position Sensor Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1)
probable_causes: Damaged cam sensor wiring or connector
Cam sensor air gap too large
Timing chain stretched causing inconsistent signal
fix_steps: Check cam sensor connector for damage or corrosion
Verify 5V reference, ground, and signal at connector
Scope cam sensor signal — should show clean square wave
Check sensor air gap and mounting
Replace sensor if signal is missing or erratic
faults.field_tricks: On some engines, the cam sensor is behind the timing cover and requires significant disassembly. Before going there, check the wiring all the way to the ECM — a lot of time gets wasted pulling timing covers for a broken wire.

faults.name: Exhaust Gas Recirculation Flow Insufficient
probable_causes: Clogged EGR passages in intake manifold
Failed EGR vacuum solenoid or electronic actuator
fix_steps: Remove EGR valve and inspect passages — clean carbon deposits
Command EGR valve open with scan tool — verify movement
Check vacuum supply to EGR valve (vacuum-operated systems)
On Ford, test DPFE sensor — voltage should change when EGR commanded open
Clean or replace EGR valve if stuck
Rod out EGR passages in intake manifold if clogged
faults.field_tricks: On Ford trucks with DPFE sensor, the rubber hoses to the DPFE sensor crack and cause this code. Replace the $3 hoses before spending $200 on a new EGR valve. Takes 5 minutes.

faults.name: Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)
probable_causes: Catalytic converter deteriorated (normal aging at 80-120K miles)
Engine misfire damaging catalyst with raw fuel
Exhaust leak before downstream O2 sensor
Downstream O2 sensor failure (mimics bad cat)
Oil or coolant contamination destroying catalyst
fix_steps: Verify no other codes present — fix misfires and lean/rich codes FIRST
Compare upstream vs downstream O2 sensor waveforms — downstream should be nearly flat
If downstream sensor mirrors upstream (switching), catalyst is failing
Check for exhaust leaks between sensors
Verify engine is not burning oil or coolant (destroys catalyst)
Replace catalytic converter if confirmed — use CARB-compliant in CA states
faults.field_tricks: Before condemning a $1000 cat, check for an exhaust leak near the downstream O2 sensor. Even a pinhole leak at a weld seam makes the downstream sensor read wrong. Tighten the downstream sensor and check all flange connections first.

faults.name: Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 2)
probable_causes: Engine running rich on bank 2 overloading catalyst
Exhaust leak near bank 2 downstream sensor
fix_steps: Same diagnostic approach as P0420 but on bank 2
Fix any rich condition on bank 2 first (P0172)
Compare upstream vs downstream O2 waveforms bank 2
Check for exhaust leaks on bank 2 side
Replace bank 2 catalytic converter if confirmed
faults.field_tricks: If both P0420 and P0430 set together, the engine problem is likely upstream — misfires, rich running, or oil burning. Fix the root cause before replacing both cats.

faults.name: Evaporative Emission System Malfunction
probable_causes: Failed purge valve (stuck open or closed)
fix_steps: Check gas cap first — tighten or replace if damaged
Inspect all EVAP hoses for cracks, especially at the charcoal canister
Smoke test EVAP system to find leaks
Test purge solenoid — should hold vacuum when de-energized, open when powered
Test vent solenoid operation
Check fuel tank pressure sensor reading
faults.field_tricks: 80% of EVAP codes are the gas cap or a cracked hose at the canister. Always start there. The smoke test is king for EVAP diag — without it you're guessing.

faults.name: Evaporative Emission System Incorrect Purge Flow
probable_causes: Purge valve stuck open or closed
fix_steps: Apply vacuum to purge valve — should hold vacuum with no power
Apply 12V to purge valve — should release vacuum (valve opens)
Inspect purge line from valve to intake manifold
Check charcoal canister for saturation (remove and shake — liquid = saturated)
Smoke test entire EVAP system
faults.field_tricks: A stuck-open purge valve on some Toyotas causes rough idle and rich running on cold start. If you have a rough cold start + EVAP code, test the purge valve first.

faults.name: Evaporative Emission System Small Leak Detected
probable_causes: Loose or worn gas cap seal
Small leak at fuel filler neck
Fuel tank O-ring leak at sender
fix_steps: Replace gas cap with OEM (aftermarket caps are the #1 cause of EVAP comebacks)
Smoke test at 0.5 PSI — look for small wisps of smoke
Check fuel filler neck for rust or cracks
Inspect fuel tank sender O-ring — access from inside trunk/under rear seat
Check vent valve for debris preventing full seal
faults.field_tricks: Small leak codes are THE most frustrating codes in automotive. Get a good smoke machine, drop the system to 0.5 PSI, and be patient. Use a mirror and flashlight under the car. Most small leaks are at rubber connections that hardened with age.

faults.name: Evaporative Emission System Purge Control Valve Circuit
probable_causes: Open or short in purge valve wiring
fix_steps: Measure purge valve solenoid resistance — typically 20-40 ohms
Check for power and ground at purge valve connector
Inspect wiring for damage
Replace purge valve if resistance is out of spec or wiring checks good
faults.field_tricks: On Hondas, the purge valve is under the intake manifold and a pain to access. Buy the part first and have it ready — you don't want to pull the manifold twice.

faults.name: Evaporative Emission System Vent Control Circuit
probable_causes: Spider webs or debris blocking canister vent port
fix_steps: Locate vent solenoid (usually near charcoal canister under vehicle)
Command vent closed with scan tool — should hear click and block airflow
Check vent filter for blockage
Measure solenoid resistance
Replace vent solenoid if stuck or no click
faults.field_tricks: Spiders love to nest in the charcoal canister vent port. Before buying any parts, pull the vent hose off the canister and look inside. A spider web blocking the vent causes this code and costs nothing to fix.

faults.name: Evaporative Emission System Vent Valve/Solenoid Circuit
probable_causes: Open or short in vent valve circuit wiring
fix_steps: Locate vent valve solenoid — usually near charcoal canister at rear of vehicle
Check connector for corrosion from road spray
Measure solenoid resistance — typically 20-40 ohms
Verify power supply and ECM ground signal
Replace vent valve if resistance out of spec
faults.field_tricks: On GM trucks, the vent solenoid is behind the rear wheel on the frame rail. Road salt and water destroy the connector every 5-7 years. Clean it up and apply dielectric grease — sometimes that's all it needs.

faults.name: Evaporative Emission System Large Leak Detected
probable_causes: Gas cap missing, loose, or cross-threaded
Fuel tank leak at sender gasket
fix_steps: Check gas cap — retighten or replace
Visual inspection of all EVAP hoses — look for disconnected or broken lines
Smoke test at 1-2 PSI — large leaks are usually easy to find
Check purge valve — if stuck open, intake vacuum will pull through system
Inspect charcoal canister for cracks
faults.field_tricks: Large leak is actually easier to find than small leak. Start with the gas cap. Then look at every hose and connection. Smoke test makes it a 5-minute job. Most common cause is someone left the gas cap off.

faults.name: Evaporative Emission System Very Small Leak Detected
probable_causes: Very small leak at fuel tank seal
fix_steps: Replace gas cap with OEM — first and cheapest fix
Low-pressure smoke test (0.5 PSI) with patience
Check all connections with soapy water if smoke machine unavailable
Inspect fuel tank top seals
May need to replace hardened hoses that pass smoke but leak under thermal cycling
faults.field_tricks: Very small leak codes often come and go with temperature. The leak may only show when the system heats up and cools down. Try smoke testing with engine at operating temp — things expand and leak that looked fine cold.

faults.name: Vehicle Speed Sensor Malfunction
probable_causes: Transmission output shaft speed sensor failure
Instrument cluster failure (if VSS signal routes through cluster)
Tone ring damage on output shaft
fix_steps: Check if speedometer works — if dead, confirms VSS issue
Locate VSS on transmission — clean connector, check wiring
Measure VSS output with scope while driving or spinning shaft
Check tone ring through sensor bore for damaged teeth
Replace VSS if signal is absent or erratic
faults.field_tricks: On older Hondas, the VSS signal goes through the instrument cluster first. If the cluster has a bad solder joint, you get P0500 and the ECM thinks the car is stopped. Resolder the cluster before replacing the VSS.

faults.name: Idle Air Control System Malfunction
probable_causes: Carbon buildup in IAC valve or throttle body bore
fix_steps: Clean throttle body and IAC passages with TB cleaner
Test IAC valve — should buzz or click when commanded
Check for vacuum leaks that would affect idle
Verify IAC wiring and connector integrity
Perform idle relearn procedure after cleaning
Replace IAC valve if cleaning doesn't restore proper operation
faults.field_tricks: Before replacing an IAC valve, clean the throttle body. 9 times out of 10, the idle problem is carbon on the throttle plate, not the IAC. Save the customer $150.

faults.name: Idle Control System RPM Higher Than Expected
probable_causes: Vacuum leak (most common cause of high idle)
Throttle body not closing fully (carbon buildup)
Fast idle thermo valve stuck (older vehicles)
fix_steps: Check for vacuum leaks with smoke test or brake cleaner spray
Clean throttle body — verify plate closes fully
Check IAC valve position — should close at warm idle
Verify no air leaks at intake manifold gasket
Reset idle after repairs — key on 10 sec, start, idle 3 min with AC off
faults.field_tricks: High idle after a repair? Check if you left a vacuum hose off. It happens to everyone. Also check if the brake booster hose is cracked — that's a massive vacuum leak that causes high idle.

faults.name: Engine Oil Pressure Sensor/Switch Circuit
probable_causes: Low oil level or low oil pressure (dangerous)
Wiring issue to oil pressure sensor
fix_steps: CHECK OIL LEVEL FIRST — low oil kills engines
If oil level is good, install mechanical oil pressure gauge to verify actual pressure
Should read 25-65 PSI at operating temp, 10+ PSI at idle
If actual pressure is good, replace oil pressure sensor
If actual pressure is low, check oil pump and pickup screen
faults.field_tricks: Never assume this is just a bad sensor. Verify with a real gauge. A $5 sensor replacement doesn't fix a $5000 engine failure. Low oil pressure + ticking noise = stop the engine immediately.

faults.name: System Voltage Low
fix_steps: Check battery voltage — should be 12.6V resting, 13.5-14.5V running
Load test battery — many auto parts stores do this free
Check alternator output with multimeter at battery while running
Inspect serpentine belt tension and condition
Clean and tighten battery terminals and chassis grounds
faults.field_tricks: Most 'alternator failures' are actually bad battery connections. Clean both terminals with a wire brush, tighten to spec, and recheck before buying a new alternator. Saves $300.

faults.name: Serial Communication Link Malfunction
probable_causes: Damaged wiring in CAN bus network
fix_steps: Check for multiple U-codes (communication loss) alongside P0600
Measure CAN bus resistance between CAN-H and CAN-L — should be 60 ohms
Inspect CAN bus wiring for damage, especially at connectors
Check for water intrusion under dash (common with leaking windshields)
If CAN bus resistance is wrong, isolate modules to find shorted unit
faults.field_tricks: If you see P0600 with a bunch of U-codes, it's almost always a CAN bus wiring issue, not the ECM. Measure 60 ohms between CAN-H and CAN-L at the DLC. If you get 120 ohms, one terminator is missing. If you get 0 ohms, something is shorted.

faults.name: Internal Control Module Memory Check Sum Error
probable_causes: Power supply issue to ECM causing memory corruption
fix_steps: Check ECM power and ground circuits first
Attempt ECM reflash/reprogram with factory software
Check for water damage at ECM location
If reflash fails, ECM replacement is needed
After ECM replacement, program VIN, mileage, and relearn all sensors
faults.field_tricks: Before spending $800+ on an ECM, try disconnecting the battery for 30 minutes and reconnecting. Some memory errors are from voltage spikes and clear themselves. Also try a reflash at the dealer — it's cheaper than a new ECM.

faults.name: Transmission Control System Malfunction
probable_causes: TCM detected an internal fault — check for specific trans codes
CAN bus communication issue between ECM and TCM
Wiring harness damage between ECM and TCM
fix_steps: P0700 is an informational code — check for accompanying specific transmission codes
Read TCM codes separately (some scan tools only read ECM by default)
Check transmission fluid level and condition
Verify CAN bus communication between modules
Address the specific transmission code that triggered P0700
faults.field_tricks: P0700 alone tells you nothing. It just says the transmission module flagged something. You MUST pull the specific transmission code (P07xx, P08xx, or manufacturer-specific) to know what's actually wrong.

faults.name: Transmission Range Sensor Circuit Malfunction (PRNDL Input)
probable_causes: Failed transmission range sensor (neutral safety switch)
Damaged wiring between range sensor and TCM
fix_steps: Check if gear indicator on dash matches actual gear selection
Inspect range sensor connector for corrosion
Adjust range sensor if out of alignment (common after trans removal)
Measure sensor resistance in each gear position — compare to spec
Replace range sensor if adjustment doesn't resolve
faults.field_tricks: After any transmission R&R, always check range sensor adjustment. If the car won't start in Park but starts in Neutral, the range sensor is off by one detent. Loosen, shift to Neutral, re-center, retighten.

faults.name: Input/Turbine Speed Sensor Circuit Malfunction
probable_causes: Failed input speed sensor inside transmission
Metal debris on sensor tip (magnetic pickup)
fix_steps: Check transmission fluid level and condition — burnt fluid = internal damage
Read input and output speed sensor data on scan tool
If input speed reads 0 while driving, sensor or wiring is bad
Inspect external wiring and connector first
If wiring OK, sensor is internal and requires pan drop or trans removal
faults.field_tricks: On Chrysler transmissions (42RE, 45RFE), the input speed sensor is on the outside of the case — easy to replace without dropping the pan. On most others, it's internal. Check the service manual before quoting labor.

faults.name: Output Speed Sensor Circuit Malfunction
probable_causes: Damaged wiring or connector at sensor
Metal debris on magnetic sensor tip
Damaged tone ring on output shaft
fix_steps: Check if speedometer works — output speed sensor often feeds speedometer
Inspect sensor connector for damage or corrosion
Measure sensor resistance — compare to spec
Clean sensor tip of any metal debris
Replace sensor — usually externally accessible on trans case
faults.field_tricks: If the speedometer is dead AND you have P0720, it's definitely the output speed sensor. If the speedo works fine, the issue may be in the circuit between the sensor and the TCM, not the sensor itself.

faults.name: Incorrect Gear Ratio
probable_causes: Failed shift solenoid not engaging gear fully
fix_steps: Check transmission fluid level and condition
Monitor input vs output speed ratio in each gear with scan tool
Compare calculated gear ratio to spec — if off, clutch packs are slipping
Check shift solenoid operation
If gear ratio is consistently wrong, internal rebuild or replacement needed
faults.field_tricks: Compare actual gear ratio to spec using input/output speed. If 1st gear should be 2.84:1 and you're seeing 2.20:1, the clutch pack is slipping. This is usually an internal repair — budget for a rebuild.

faults.name: Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Malfunction
probable_causes: Wiring issue in internal trans harness
Sticking TCC apply valve in valve body
fix_steps: Check for shudder at highway speed (TCC engagement)
Monitor TCC slip with scan tool — should be near 0 RPM when engaged
Check TCC solenoid resistance through external connector
Inspect trans fluid for debris or burnt smell
Drop pan and inspect valve body for stuck TCC apply valve
faults.field_tricks: TCC shudder at 40-50 mph lockup is very common. Try a full fluid flush and replace with the correct OEM-spec fluid (not generic ATF). Some transmissions are extremely fluid-sensitive — wrong fluid causes shudder.

faults.name: Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Performance/Stuck Off
probable_causes: TCC solenoid stuck off or weak
TCC apply circuit restricted in valve body
Wiring issue preventing TCC solenoid activation
fix_steps: Monitor TCC slip on scan tool — high slip when commanded on = not engaging
Command TCC on manually with scan tool — watch for engagement
Check TCC solenoid function
Inspect valve body for stuck TCC apply valve or clogged passages
If converter clutch material is worn, replace torque converter
faults.field_tricks: Before pulling the transmission for a converter, try commanding the TCC on with a scan tool. If it engages when commanded but not automatically, the issue is in the TCM logic or a sensor — not the converter.

faults.name: Shift Solenoid A Malfunction
probable_causes: Failed shift solenoid A (stuck or electrically open)
Valve body issue preventing solenoid function
fix_steps: Check transmission fluid condition — burnt or dark = internal contamination
Measure solenoid resistance through external connector
Command solenoid on/off with scan tool — listen for click
Drop pan and inspect solenoid for debris
Replace solenoid pack if individual solenoid not serviceable
Flush and refill with correct spec fluid
faults.field_tricks: On most modern transmissions, solenoids come as a pack (solenoid body). You can't replace just one. Budget for the full pack plus fluid. While you have the pan off, replace the filter too.

faults.name: System Too Lean (Bank 1)
probable_causes: Vacuum leak (intake manifold gasket, PCV hose, brake booster line)
Weak fuel pump or clogged fuel filter
Dirty or failed MAF sensor reading low
fix_steps: Check long-term fuel trim at idle and at 2500 RPM
Smoke test intake for vacuum leaks
Clean MAF sensor with MAF cleaner
Check fuel pressure
Inspect PCV valve, brake booster hose, and all small vacuum lines
faults.field_tricks: LTFT high at idle but normal at cruise = vacuum leak. LTFT high everywhere = fuel delivery. That one rule saves hours.

faults.name: Lack of HO2S Switch — Sensor Indicates Lean (Ford)
probable_causes: Exhaust leak before upstream O2 sensor
fix_steps: Check long-term fuel trim bank 1 — high positive = lean
Smoke test for vacuum leaks around intake manifold
Monitor O2 sensor waveform — should toggle between rich and lean
Check fuel pressure — 35-45 PSI at idle on Ford EFI
Inspect intake manifold runner control for binding
faults.field_tricks: Ford-specific code. On 4.6L and 5.4L, check the intake manifold runner flaps (IMRC). If they're stuck or the actuator is broken, bank 1 runs lean. The actuator rod end pops off the ball stud.

faults.name: Lack of HO2S Switch — Sensor Indicates Lean (Ford Bank 2)
probable_causes: Exhaust leak before bank 2 upstream O2
fix_steps: Same approach as P1131 but on bank 2
Compare LTFT bank 1 vs bank 2
Smoke test bank 2 intake runners
Check fuel pressure under load
Replace O2 sensor only after confirming no lean condition
faults.field_tricks: If both P1131 and P1151 set together on a Ford, it's a system-wide lean condition — fuel pressure, MAF, or a big vacuum leak. Don't replace O2 sensors until you find the root cause.

faults.name: VTEC System Malfunction (Honda)
probable_causes: Low oil pressure at VTEC solenoid
fix_steps: Check oil level and condition — change if overdue
Remove VTEC solenoid and inspect screen for sludge
Clean screen with brake cleaner or replace solenoid
Test VTEC solenoid valve — apply 12V, should click and pass oil
Check VTEC pressure switch operation
faults.field_tricks: Honda VTEC code is almost always the solenoid screen clogged with sludge. Pull the solenoid off the head, pop out the little screen, clean it with brake cleaner, reinstall. 20-minute fix that most shops charge for a full solenoid replacement.

faults.name: Crankshaft/Camshaft Position Correlation (GM)
probable_causes: Distributor out of position (older GM V8)
Cam sensor out of time with crank
fix_steps: Set engine to TDC #1 compression stroke
Check distributor rotor position — should point to #1 terminal
Loosen distributor and adjust cam retard angle on scan tool to 0 degrees
If no distributor (LS engine), check timing chain stretch
Verify cam sensor correlation with crank sensor using scope
faults.field_tricks: On GM 5.3L/5.7L/4.3L with distributor, this code means the distributor is one tooth off. Set #1 TDC, loosen distributor, watch cam retard on scan tool, set to 0 degrees, tighten. 10-minute fix.

faults.name: Random Misfire (Honda-Specific)
probable_causes: Worn distributor cap and rotor (older Hondas)
EGR valve stuck open or leaking
fix_steps: Check valve lash — Honda engines need periodic valve adjustment
Inspect distributor cap and rotor for carbon tracking
Test EGR valve — command closed, check for improvement
Inspect ignition components
If high mileage, perform intake valve cleaning
faults.field_tricks: Honda's are the ONLY common passenger cars that still need valve adjustment. Tight intake valves cause misfire at idle and can burn valves. Check clearance every 60K miles on any Honda 4-cylinder.

faults.name: Throttle Actuator Control Module Throttle Actuator Position Performance (GM)
probable_causes: Carbon buildup on electronic throttle body bore and plate
Throttle body needs relearn after cleaning or replacement
fix_steps: Clean throttle body bore and plate with TB cleaner
Perform throttle body relearn: key on 10 seconds, start, idle 3 minutes, turn off, restart
Check APP sensor readings — both sensors should track smoothly
If cleaning and relearn don't fix it, replace throttle body assembly
Verify 5V reference and grounds at TB connector
faults.field_tricks: On GM trucks and SUVs, this code with reduced power is almost always a dirty throttle body. Clean it, do the relearn, and it's fixed. Don't sell a throttle body until you've cleaned and relearned first.

faults.name: Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Lean (Bank 1)
probable_causes: Exhaust leak between catalytic converter and downstream O2 sensor
fix_steps: Check for exhaust leaks at converter flange and downstream pipe
Monitor downstream O2 sensor — should be relatively flat around 0.5-0.7V
Compare upstream and downstream O2 signals
Check air injection system if equipped
Replace downstream O2 sensor if biased lean with no leaks present
faults.field_tricks: This one is tricky. Check the downstream O2 sensor bung for being loose first. A loose sensor lets air in and reads lean without any actual exhaust leak. Retorque the sensor to 30-37 ft-lbs.

faults.name: Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Rich (Bank 1)
probable_causes: Downstream O2 sensor contaminated or failing
Catalytic converter saturated from rich running
Oil burning past rings or valve seals contaminating converter
Leaking fuel injector downstream of converter
fix_steps: Check for engine oil consumption — blue smoke from exhaust
Monitor downstream O2 sensor voltage — stuck high indicates rich or contaminated sensor
Verify no active upstream rich condition (P0172)
Inspect catalyst for contamination
Replace downstream O2 sensor if contaminated
faults.field_tricks: If the engine is burning oil and the downstream O2 reads rich, fixing the oil consumption fixes the code. Replacing the sensor is a waste if oil is still getting into the exhaust.

faults.name: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor Voltage Correlation
probable_causes: Failed throttle position sensor in throttle body
5V reference circuit shorted between TPS and APP
Wiring short or ground fault in throttle control circuit
Water intrusion in pedal assembly connector
fix_steps: Check both APP sensor voltages — should be within correlation spec at all positions
Check TPS sensor voltages in throttle body — two sensors must agree
Verify 5V reference is clean and stable (no noise or dropout)
Inspect wiring from APP to ECM and TPS to ECM for shorts
Replace APP sensor assembly if correlation error is at pedal side
Replace throttle body if correlation error is at TB side
faults.field_tricks: This code causes limp mode (reduced engine power). The car limits to ~1500 RPM. Check the pedal connector first — water intrusion is common on driver's side floor. A wet connector causes intermittent correlation faults.

faults.name: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor D/E Voltage Correlation
probable_causes: Wiring damage in APP sensor harness
Floor mat pressing on accelerator pedal causing abnormal range
Water damage to pedal sensor connector
fix_steps: Verify floor mats are not interfering with pedal movement
Check APP sensor D and E voltages — should track proportionally
Inspect pedal sensor connector for water, corrosion, or bent pins
Measure voltages while slowly pressing pedal — look for dropouts or jumps
Replace APP sensor assembly if voltages don't track correctly
faults.field_tricks: Check the floor mat first. Seriously. An aftermarket floor mat that's too thick or not secured can press on the pedal and cause this code. Pull the mat, clear the code, and test drive before ordering parts.

faults.name: O2 Sensor Signal Stuck Lean (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
probable_causes: Exhaust leak at downstream sensor location
Wiring issue — signal wire shorted to ground
fix_steps: Check for exhaust leaks at downstream sensor and converter flange
Monitor downstream O2 voltage — should read 0.5-0.8V on a healthy catalyst
If stuck below 0.2V, check wiring for short to ground
Replace downstream O2 sensor if confirmed stuck lean
Recheck after replacement — may also have catalyst issue
faults.field_tricks: A downstream sensor stuck lean usually means either the sensor is dead or there's an exhaust leak. Check the sensor gasket or sealing washer — if it's missing, outside air gets in and reads lean.

faults.name: Fuel Rail Pressure Too Low
probable_causes: Low-pressure fuel pump weak or failing
Leaking fuel injector or fuel rail seal
fix_steps: Check low-side fuel pressure first (tank pump) — should be 55+ PSI
Monitor high-side rail pressure on scan tool — compare desired vs actual
Check fuel filter if accessible (many modern cars have non-serviceable filters in tank)
Listen for high-pressure pump operation at crank — mechanical ticking
Replace high-pressure fuel pump if output is low under load
faults.field_tricks: On GDI engines, the high-pressure pump is driven by a cam lobe. Check the cam lobe and pump follower for wear — a worn lobe causes low rail pressure at high RPM that you won't catch at idle.

faults.name: Fuel Rail Pressure Too High
probable_causes: Faulty fuel rail pressure sensor reading low (causing over-compensation)
High-pressure pump control solenoid stuck active
ECM issue commanding too much fuel pressure
fix_steps: Monitor rail pressure on scan tool — if actual exceeds desired by 500+ PSI, regulator or control issue
Check fuel return line for restrictions or kinks
Verify fuel rail pressure sensor accuracy with known-good gauge
Check high-pressure pump control solenoid — should modulate, not be stuck on
Replace fuel pressure regulator if return line is clear and sensor is accurate
faults.field_tricks: Over-pressure in a GDI fuel rail is dangerous — injectors can stick open and hydrolock the engine. If you see rail pressure over 2500 PSI (most systems target 1500-2200), stop the engine and fix it before running.

faults.name: Manifold Absolute Pressure Sensor Range/Performance
probable_causes: Vacuum hose to MAP sensor cracked or disconnected
MAP sensor contaminated with oil or carbon
Intake manifold vacuum leak affecting MAP reading
Altitude change if code set during mountain driving
fix_steps: Inspect vacuum hose from MAP sensor to intake manifold
Read MAP sensor value at KOEO — should read barometric pressure (~100 kPa at sea level)
At idle, MAP should read 25-35 kPa (high vacuum)
Snap throttle — MAP should spike to near barometric then drop back
Replace MAP sensor if values don't change correctly
faults.field_tricks: If the MAP reads barometric at idle (same as key on engine off), you have a massive vacuum leak or the hose fell off the sensor. If it reads 0 at idle, the sensor is shorted internally.

faults.name: Engine Coolant Temperature Circuit High Input
probable_causes: Open circuit in ECT sensor wiring
fix_steps: Check ECT sensor connector — make sure plugged in
Measure ECT resistance — should be 2-3k ohms at 70F, 200-300 ohms at 200F
Verify 5V reference at sensor connector
Check wiring for breaks or corrosion
Replace sensor if open circuit confirmed
faults.field_tricks: ECT high input means the ECM sees extremely cold temp (below -40F). That's an open circuit signature. Check the connector first — they love to vibrate loose.

faults.name: Insufficient Coolant Temperature for Closed Loop Fuel Control
probable_causes: Low coolant level causing slow warmup
Short trips — engine never fully warms up
fix_steps: Monitor ECT on scan tool during warmup — should reach 190F+ within 5-8 minutes
If temp plateaus low, replace thermostat
Verify ECT sensor accuracy with infrared thermometer comparison
Check coolant level and bleed air from system
faults.field_tricks: This often sets in cold climates when people only drive 5 minutes. The engine never reaches operating temp. A grill cover or winter front can help. But if it sets in summer, it's the thermostat for sure.

faults.name: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Malfunction (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
probable_causes: Failed upstream O2 sensor heater bank 2
Wiring damage in bank 2 O2 harness
fix_steps: Check O2 heater fuse
Measure bank 2 upstream O2 heater resistance — 2-15 ohms typical
Inspect connector for heat damage
Check for battery voltage supply to heater circuit
Replace O2 sensor if heater element is open
faults.field_tricks: Bank 2 sensor 1 is usually the one on the passenger side (most transverse engines are opposite). Verify which bank is which before buying the sensor — putting the wrong one in costs you another trip.

faults.name: Cylinder 5 Misfire Detected
probable_causes: Intake manifold runner leak near cylinder 5
fix_steps: Coil swap test — move cyl 5 coil to another cylinder
If misfire follows, replace coil
Check spark plug gap and condition
Test injector with noid light
Compression test if ignition and fuel check out
faults.field_tricks: On Ford 4.6L/5.4L, cylinder 5 is passenger side rear and hard to access. Use a ratcheting wrench for the coil bolt. Don't drop the bolt into the valley — use a magnetic pickup.

faults.name: Cylinder 6 Misfire Detected
fix_steps: Coil swap test
Spark plug inspection and replacement
Injector noid light test
Compression test
Check valve train noise on cylinder 6 with stethoscope
faults.field_tricks: On GM 3.6L V6, cylinder 6 misfires at high mileage are often a collapsed lifter. Listen with a mechanic's stethoscope on the valve cover — a rhythmic ticking is the giveaway.

faults.name: Cylinder 7 Misfire Detected
probable_causes: Worn spark plug (rear cylinder runs hotter)
fix_steps: Coil swap test to adjacent cylinder
Check spark plug — rear cylinders often wear faster
Test injector operation
Compression and leak-down test if needed
faults.field_tricks: Cylinder 7 on a V8 is usually driver side rear — right against the firewall. Plan your tool approach before you start. A swivel spark plug socket and a 12-inch extension save a lot of frustration.

faults.name: Cylinder 8 Misfire Detected
fix_steps: Coil swap test
Spark plug inspection
Injector test
Compression test if ignition and fuel are good
Leak-down test to identify valve vs ring failure
faults.field_tricks: On Ford 5.4L 3-valve, cylinders 7 and 8 are notorious for spark plug blowout (the thread strips out of the aluminum head). If the plug is loose, you need a thread repair kit (Time-Sert). Don't force it.

faults.name: Camshaft Position Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
probable_causes: Timing chain stretch causing signal deviation
VVT system affecting cam position outside expected range
fix_steps: Compare cam sensor signal to crank sensor with scope
Check for timing chain slack
Verify cam sensor mounting and air gap
Test VVT solenoid operation
Replace cam sensor if signal is erratic
faults.field_tricks: If this code comes with P0016 (crank/cam correlation), the timing chain has probably jumped. Don't replace just the sensor — check the chain and tensioner first.

faults.name: Secondary Air Injection System Incorrect Flow Detected
probable_causes: Check valve stuck closed preventing air flow
Clogged air injection passages in cylinder head
Carbon buildup blocking air injection ports
fix_steps: Listen for air pump operation on cold start — should run for 1-2 minutes
Check air pump fuse and relay
Verify check valve allows air flow in one direction only
Inspect air injection passages in exhaust ports for carbon blockage
Replace air pump if motor doesn't spin
faults.field_tricks: On Subaru boxers, the air injection ports in the heads clog with carbon. You can rod them out with a coat hanger through the check valve port. Saves removing the heads.

faults.name: Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)
probable_causes: Oil or coolant contamination of catalyst
fix_steps: Fix any other codes first — misfires and lean/rich destroy cats
Compare upstream vs downstream O2 waveforms
Check for exhaust leaks
Verify no oil or coolant consumption
Replace cat if confirmed failed — CARB-compliant in CA
faults.field_tricks: Before condemning a $1000 cat, check downstream O2 bung for being loose. Even a pinhole leak at a weld makes the sensor read wrong. Tighten sensor and check flanges first.

faults.name: Evaporative Emission System High Purge Flow
probable_causes: Purge valve leaking through when closed
Wiring issue holding purge valve open
fix_steps: Remove purge valve vacuum line — cap the intake port
If idle quality improves with purge blocked, valve is leaking through
Apply vacuum to purge valve with no power — should hold vacuum
If it doesn't hold vacuum, valve is leaking and needs replacement
Check wiring — shorted signal wire can hold valve open
faults.field_tricks: A stuck-open purge valve floods the engine with fuel vapor. Symptoms: rich idle, stalling when warm, gas smell. Pinch the purge line — if idle gets better, that's your problem. $25 part.

faults.name: Engine Oil Pressure Sensor Range/Performance
probable_causes: Wiring issue to oil pressure sensor
Actual oil pressure fluctuation (intermittent pump issue)
fix_steps: Install mechanical gauge to verify actual oil pressure
Compare scan tool oil pressure reading to mechanical gauge
If mechanical pressure is good, replace sensor
If mechanical pressure fluctuates, check oil pump and relief valve
Verify correct oil viscosity for engine
faults.field_tricks: Most of the time this is the sensor, especially on GM trucks with the sensor on the back of the engine near the firewall. It's a pain to access but a $20 sensor fixes it 90% of the time.

faults.name: ECM/PCM Processor Fault
fix_steps: Check ECM power supply and ground circuits — measure voltage at ECM connectors
Inspect ECM for water damage or corrosion
Attempt ECM reflash/reprogram with factory tool
Check for aftermarket devices on CAN bus that could cause interference
Replace ECM if reflash fails — program VIN, mileage, immobilizer
faults.field_tricks: Before buying a $1000 ECM, check ALL ground connections. A bad ECM ground causes processor faults, communication errors, and all kinds of phantom codes. Clean and tighten every ground you can find.

faults.name: Torque Converter Clutch Performance or Stuck Off
probable_causes: TCC solenoid weak or stuck off
fix_steps: Check transmission fluid level and condition
Monitor TCC slip on scan tool — should be near 0 when engaged
Command TCC on with scan tool and watch for engagement
If no engagement, check solenoid and valve body
Replace torque converter if clutch material worn
faults.field_tricks: Try a full fluid change with correct OEM-spec fluid before condemning the converter. The wrong ATF fluid causes TCC shudder and engagement issues on many transmissions.

faults.name: System Too Rich (Bank 1)
fix_steps: Check fuel pressure at spec
Test purge valve — block off and check STFT
Clean MAF sensor
Verify ECT reads correct operating temp
Injector leak-down test
faults.field_tricks: Pull dipstick. If it smells like gas, you've got a leaking injector or bad pressure regulator. Change the oil after fixing it — gas-diluted oil kills bearings.

faults.name: OBD System Readiness Test Not Complete (Ford)
probable_causes: Not enough drive cycles completed since reset
fix_steps: This is not a fault — it means readiness monitors need to run
Complete a drive cycle: cold start, idle 2 min, accelerate to 55 mph, cruise 5 min, decel without braking, idle
Check which monitors are incomplete with scan tool
Some monitors require specific conditions (fuel level 15-85%, ambient temp above 40F)
May take 2-3 drive cycles to set all monitors
faults.field_tricks: For emissions testing: you need all monitors complete (or at most 1 incomplete on older vehicles). Ford's drive cycle is picky — fuel level must be between 15-85% and the car has to be cold-started. Tell the customer NOT to top off the tank before the smog test.

faults.name: MAF Sensor Out of Self Test Range (Ford/GM)
fix_steps: Clean MAF sensor with MAF cleaner
Inspect intake boot for cracks — especially accordion section
Smoke test from MAF to throttle body for leaks
Compare MAF reading to spec at idle and snap throttle
Replace MAF if cleaning doesn't resolve
faults.field_tricks: On GM, P1101 usually means the MAF and MAP disagree. Clean the MAF first. If that doesn't fix it, look for a vacuum leak between the MAF and the intake manifold — that's where the readings diverge.

faults.name: O2 Sensor Signal Stuck Lean (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
probable_causes: Failed upstream O2 sensor stuck lean
fix_steps: Check fuel trims — if high positive, system is genuinely lean
Smoke test for vacuum leaks
Check fuel pressure under load
Clean or replace MAF sensor
Replace O2 sensor only after confirming no lean condition exists
faults.field_tricks: Stuck lean means the ECM has maxed out its fuel correction trying to compensate. Fix the lean condition first — replacing the O2 sensor won't help if the engine is actually running lean.

faults.name: O2 Sensor Signal Stuck Rich (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
probable_causes: Coolant temperature sensor reading falsely cold
fix_steps: Check fuel trims — if high negative, system is genuinely rich
Test purge valve — block off purge line
Check fuel pressure against spec
Verify ECT sensor accuracy
Replace O2 sensor only after confirming no rich condition
faults.field_tricks: If the O2 sensor reads above 0.9V continuously, try unplugging the sensor. If the code changes from 'stuck rich' to 'low voltage,' the sensor was the problem. If it stays rich, the engine is actually over-fueling.

faults.name: Fuel Level Sensor Circuit High Input
probable_causes: Wiring issue — short to voltage or open ground
fix_steps: Check fuel gauge reading — stuck on full suggests high input
Measure fuel level sensor resistance at fuel pump connector
Most senders: empty = 230 ohms, full = 33 ohms (varies by make)
If resistance is stuck low, sender float arm may be stuck or sender failed
Replace fuel level sender — usually requires dropping tank or accessing through trunk
faults.field_tricks: Before dropping the tank, check the connector at the fuel pump module first. A lot of trucks have an access panel under the rear seat or bed. GM trucks have it under the bed — cut a hole in the bed liner to save dropping the tank.

faults.name: Manifold Absolute Pressure Sensor Circuit High Input
probable_causes: MAP sensor vacuum hose disconnected or cracked
Large vacuum leak making MAP read high
fix_steps: Check MAP vacuum hose — reconnect if disconnected
Read MAP sensor value — should be 95-100 kPa at KOEO, 25-35 kPa at idle
If reading stays at 100+ kPa at idle, vacuum hose is off or sensor is bad
Check 5V reference for overvoltage
Replace MAP sensor if hose is good and reading is stuck high
faults.field_tricks: MAP high input = the ECM thinks there's no vacuum. Most of the time the hose just fell off or cracked. Look at the sensor on the intake manifold and check the little rubber hose. 30-second fix.

faults.name: Camshaft Position Sensor Range/Performance (Bank 1)
probable_causes: Timing chain stretch causing signal deviation
fix_steps: Compare cam signal to crank signal with scope
Check timing chain for excess slack
Verify VVT solenoid operation
Check sensor mounting and gap
Replace sensor if signal is erratic with good chain
faults.field_tricks: If this code accompanies P0016, suspect timing chain stretch first. On high-mileage engines, the chain and sensor fail at the same time.

faults.name: Ignition Coil A Primary/Secondary Circuit
probable_causes: Failed ignition coil A (cylinder 1 on most engines)
Coil driver circuit in ECM failed
Wiring open or short in coil primary circuit
Spark plug gap too wide overloading coil
fix_steps: Measure coil primary resistance — typically 0.3-1.0 ohms
Measure secondary resistance — typically 5,000-15,000 ohms
Check for power supply at coil connector (battery voltage)
Verify ECM trigger signal with scope or test light
Swap coil with known good — if code moves, coil is bad
faults.field_tricks: COP (coil-on-plug) coils fail from heat cycling and spark plug gap wear. When replacing a coil, always replace the spark plug at the same time. A worn plug with wide gap kills the new coil fast.

faults.name: Exhaust Camshaft Position Timing Over-Advanced (Bank 1)
probable_causes: Low oil pressure to exhaust cam phaser
Sludge in oil passages to exhaust phaser
Stretched timing chain affecting exhaust cam timing
fix_steps: Check oil level and condition — change if needed
Monitor exhaust cam timing on scan tool
Command exhaust VVT solenoid off — timing should return to base
Remove and inspect exhaust VVT solenoid screen for debris
Replace exhaust cam phaser if solenoid and oil confirmed good
faults.field_tricks: Same root cause as P0011 but on the exhaust side. Oil maintenance is everything on these VVT engines. Run full synthetic, change at 5000 miles, and these codes go away.

faults.name: Exhaust Gas Recirculation Circuit Range/Performance
probable_causes: Carbon buildup in EGR valve preventing full range of motion
fix_steps: Remove EGR valve and inspect for carbon buildup
Clean EGR valve thoroughly with carbon cleaner
Command EGR valve through full range with scan tool
Check position sensor feedback — should track command smoothly
Replace EGR valve if cleaning doesn't restore full range
faults.field_tricks: Soak the EGR valve in carbon cleaner overnight. Most of the time the motor works fine once the carbon is removed. A $3 can of carbon cleaner saves a $200 EGR valve.

faults.name: Evaporative Emission System Leak Detected (Fuel Cap Loose/Off)
probable_causes: Gas cap loose, missing, or cross-threaded
Gas cap seal damaged or deteriorated
Aftermarket gas cap not sealing properly
Fuel filler neck damaged or corroded
fix_steps: Remove and reinstall gas cap — make sure it clicks 3+ times
Inspect gas cap seal for cracks or deformation
Replace with OEM gas cap if seal is damaged
Check filler neck for rust or dents (trucks especially)
Clear code and drive 2 cycles — monitor for return
faults.field_tricks: Chrysler/Dodge/Jeep set this code specifically when they detect a fuel cap issue. Replace the cap with OEM Mopar — the aftermarket caps from the parts store have a 50% comeback rate.

faults.name: Idle Speed Higher Than Expected
probable_causes: Dirty throttle body not closing fully
AC compressor cycling issue affecting idle
fix_steps: Smoke test for vacuum leaks
Clean throttle body plate and bore
Check IAC valve operation
Check PCV valve — replace if stuck open
Perform idle relearn after repairs
faults.field_tricks: After cleaning a drive-by-wire throttle body, the idle WILL be high until you do a relearn. Every car is different — some need a scan tool, some just need key-on-engine-off for 30 seconds then idle for 3 minutes.

faults.name: Fuel System Too Lean (Bank 1)
probable_causes: Vacuum leak at intake manifold or vacuum lines
Low fuel pressure from weak pump or clogged filter
Leaking PCV valve or crankcase ventilation hose
Cracked or aged intake manifold (plastic designs)
fix_steps: Compare LTFT at idle vs 2500 RPM — idle-only lean = vacuum leak, all-RPM lean = fuel system
Smoke test intake for leaks
Clean MAF with MAF cleaner only
Check fuel pressure — 55-62 PSI typical for port injection
Inspect plastic intake manifolds for cracks
faults.field_tricks: The LTFT idle vs cruise comparison is the single most valuable diagnostic technique for lean codes. Learn it, use it every time.

faults.name: Unable to Bleed Up Fuel Tank Vacuum (Ford)
probable_causes: Pinched or kinked EVAP hose between vent and tank
Fuel tank deformation from excess vacuum
fix_steps: Listen for fuel tank being under vacuum when opening gas cap (loud 'whoosh')
Locate canister vent solenoid — usually on charcoal canister near rear of vehicle
Command vent solenoid open with scan tool — listen for click
Measure solenoid resistance — compare to spec
Replace vent solenoid if stuck closed
faults.field_tricks: If you hear a loud rush of air when opening the gas cap on a Ford, that's the symptom. The fuel tank is collapsing under vacuum because the vent can't open. Common on Ford trucks — the vent solenoid is on the charcoal canister mounted to the frame.

faults.name: Throttle Actuator Control Motor Circuit Range/Performance
probable_causes: Wiring issue to throttle body motor
5V reference circuit shared with other sensors dropping out
fix_steps: Clean throttle body — carbon binding is cause #1
Check throttle body motor connector for corrosion
Verify 5V reference is stable at throttle body connector
Command throttle open/close with scan tool — watch for binding or skipping
Replace throttle body assembly if motor is weak or binding persists after cleaning
faults.field_tricks: This code triggers limp mode on most vehicles. Cleaning the throttle body resolves it 70% of the time. Do the relearn procedure after cleaning or it won't idle right.

faults.name: Cooling Fan 1 Control Circuit
fix_steps: Check cooling fan fuse and relay first
Command fan on with scan tool — listen for operation
Apply 12V directly to fan motor to test (bypass relay)
Check wiring from relay to fan motor
Replace relay or fan motor as needed
faults.field_tricks: Swap the fan relay with another identical relay in the fuse box (horn relay is usually the same). If the fan runs with the swapped relay, you found a $10 fix.

faults.name: Under Boost Condition
probable_causes: Boost leak in intercooler piping or hoses
Turbocharger shaft play or bearing failure
fix_steps: Check all intercooler piping and hose clamps for boost leaks
Pressure test intake system to 15 PSI — listen for leaks
Check wastegate actuator — apply vacuum/pressure, verify movement
Inspect turbo shaft for radial and axial play
Verify boost pressure sensor reading at atmosphere = barometric
faults.field_tricks: Boost leaks are the #1 cause of underboost. The silicone couplers at the intercooler blow off under load and pop back on — you won't see it at idle. Pressure test the system or have someone watch the pipes while you hit boost.

faults.name: Turbocharger/Supercharger Overboost Condition
probable_causes: Boost control solenoid stuck open (overriding wastegate)
Wastegate actuator rod disconnected or broken
Boost pressure sensor reading low (PCM overcompensates)
Aftermarket tune with boost too high
fix_steps: CHECK IMMEDIATELY — overboost can damage engine internals
Inspect wastegate actuator and rod — should move freely
Check boost control solenoid — disconnect and test wastegate without it
Verify boost pressure sensor accuracy
If aftermarket tune, revert to stock and retest
faults.field_tricks: Overboost is dangerous. If the wastegate rod end popped off the ball stud, boost is uncontrolled. Pull over and check before driving further. Zip-tie the rod end as a temporary fix to get home.

faults.name: EGR Flow Insufficient
probable_causes: Failed EGR vacuum solenoid or actuator
fix_steps: Remove and clean EGR valve and passages
Command EGR open with scan tool
Check vacuum to EGR on vacuum systems
Test DPFE sensor (Ford) — hoses crack
Replace if stuck after cleaning
faults.field_tricks: Ford DPFE hoses crack every 5 years. Replace the $3 rubber hoses before spending $200 on an EGR valve.

faults.name: System Lean Bank 1
fix_steps: Check LTFT at idle vs 2500 RPM
Smoke test intake
Clean MAF
Check fuel pressure
Inspect all vacuum lines
faults.field_tricks: LTFT high at idle but normal at cruise = vacuum leak. LTFT high everywhere = fuel delivery.
